sentences.text: And-- and everything healed.
Three days later, when she [? looked ?] [? at it, ?] she said, everything is perfect.
Yes.
So I was already in concentration.
They were bringing the lunch.
It was in the dark.
Nine months were in the dark there.
And I fell down.
We were there.
And I asked her, why is-- sending me to work back?
She said, they're going to give you light work, not the hard work.
And I said to her, have pity.
She said she was a prisoner herself.
Anyway, I went back to work.
They gave me a light work and everything.
Later [INAUDIBLE].
Are they gonna send away some people in another-- in another camp just a couple months later-- was a-- are we gonna send away another camp in this camp prisoners who were there with us, killers?
And these were the big-- you cannot run no place.
It's the end of the world, Eskimos live there.
Anyway, and they say, there you finish your life.
There they-- they get you out.
All of a sudden I came home from work, three Russian standing soldiers and they take me.
Go in and get your clothes and come with us.
And I said to him in Russian, where are you taking me?
Are you taking me to-- he would not talk to me.
We were walking for a half an hour and we got to a place and I said, oh my God.
Thousands of soldiers, Polish [INAUDIBLE].
And they say, you go and be free.
Started to go to the camp.
We're gonna be free.
I said, what?
We're gonna be free.
I never believed so many people are there.
When I was in my camp, I don't know, I ran all over there with all the soldiers, hundreds of thousands-- Polacks.
Not just Poles-- Jews, everything from Poland.
Anyway, but one thing.
My best friend-- when they ask you your life's story, when they ask you where you were born, where you-- everything, I always tell the truth.
And this guys, when he was arrested, he tell he was born in Germany.
Germany was their allies with-- with-- with Russia.
You see, they attacked Poland.
And he said he's a German instead of a Polack.
When we were free, he was not called to be free.
He's a German.
When Germany attacked the--
Uh-huh.
Russia.
It was no good.
Yes.
When Germany attacked Russia, we don't know about it.
And we-- we were free.
America supplied with the food.
And we were sitting there for a week, till they-- they get the papers to see if you're the right person and everything.
They give you-- and this guy was crying, I lied.
I'm from Poland.
I'm this.
I don't know what happened to him.
They give us money, and they give us food, and we were going to the army from the Polish Army.
Polish Army.
Sure.
When Hitler attacked Russia, the Polish General Sikorski-- General Sikorski flew from London to Moscow.
He made a pact with Stalin to leave the-- all the Polish prisoners in the army.
And the-- and the-- be allies.
America supplied the food, the uniforms and we went from the North Pole to the Far East, the tropical.
[INAUDIBLE] was the place, near Tashkent.
You hear about Tashkent, Samarkand, Uzbekistan near the Iranian border there climate.
They are the Arabs, but they're not the Arabs.
The are Uzbeks.
They call them Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan.
We were there and the tanks was going-- people were free.
Oh, trains.
We got the trains, special trains.
Oh, for a week or more.
In every state, they had to bring food and they stopped and they changed the locomotive, the-- the trains.
Anyway, we got to Samarkand--
Well, in the trains we were already American food.
We went to bakeries and everything and they would get the bread.
We had good food.
America supplied the food.
For a year and half.
About a year and a half.
So now it's about 19--
When Hitler attacked-- when Hitler attacked Russia, we were free.
Close to '42, yeah.
But anyway-- and I know my brothers was Avraham [INAUDIBLE].
As a city-- the family, they arrest him and they threw him with his wife and children and my brother, who-- who was with them.
As a family, they put them together and they sent him there to work.
He was not onto the-- by [INAUDIBLE] the guns.
And [INAUDIBLE] could not move there.
And they released all the prisoners from-- all the people from there, too.
And every time we stopped and I said, let me see-- my brother had two children.
And I see-- and I-- we-- we had a couple of hours walk around in the station.
And I went-- right there by the station was orphans for children.
And I walked over to them, Polish children, and I said, you know my-- my-- my nieces were here and everything?
They didn't know nothing.
My brother was every day by this station, my older brother.
And he know--
Yeah.
My older brother, he was there.
Zanwel, ?] he was here with his wife and children and their two brothers, when they were released, they lived there.
And I walked to the station.
I walk and I-- and I-- and I-- and-- and [INAUDIBLE] for to supply the food to the army, everything.
And my brother oldest was every day looking for me.
And the moment that my train came in, he went home.
And I meet a guy and I said, are you-- was in camp?
He said, yes.
Where?
[INAUDIBLE].
I said, you was with my brother?
He said, what's your brother?
I said, Galek.
Oh, yes.
I was with him.
Your brother was here.
Just now he went home.
[LAUGHS]
And I had to go with the army.
And I told him, look, my brothers lived in [INAUDIBLE].
I want to see him.
I want to make a [? decision. ?] And-- and the-- the army, they know me.
I was playing soccer.
They know me.
And they said to me, look, where are you going?
Leave Russia.
Don't worry about it.
We are going-- soon we get to the army, we go into Iran and everything.
I said, no.
I want to see my brother.
They gave me a pass to get a train and go there for two weeks.
And if you decide to stay there, we cannot force you.
You stay there.
I came back and I-- and my brother was waiting in a train there.
I came back and he took me to his house.
He walked into his wife and said, I got-- I guessed.
She didn't know.
She-- she was born in the same city, went to school with me.
Oh, she started to scream.
And my brother said, you're not going back.
You stay with us.
Thank God [INAUDIBLE].
I save my brother's life and the children.
And-- and I got a job with their work.
I went back and I told them, I'm sorry.
With my brother's work.
In [INAUDIBLE] supplying food to the army and everything.
My brother was afraid for anything.
He worked in a meat-- he was a-- he was a butcher at home.
He worked by cutting meat.
And there in Russia, you cannot live.
You have to steal anything.
But when you steal something, they arrest you and they put you in jail.
And I got contact with the police right away.
I got contact with the police and with the judges and everything.
And I said, you want to make money, to the police.
Yeah.
I-- look, two people together.
He can do nothing.
He said, yes.
And later on I-- I had a girlfriend there, I was going out and I-- and the judge, we were going out dancing and everything.
And I used to take out whole cows.
I took it out.
The guy who stayed in the factory, he-- he was paid, too-- everything.
Later on, I was working in the meat in the black market.
[INAUDIBLE] people came to the house and bought the meat and everything from black market.
And I took the money and I split it with the judge, with the--
--police with the guy who was the guard and everything.
We were [? getting ?] 100 rubles for a pound of meat.
I said, I'm gonna quit this.
I used to be delivering the-- the-- the food to the army and everything.
I'm gonna go to a tea factory.
Yeah.
A tea-- [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] the tea-- they-- they-- they take tea, the Arabs and everything.
And the tea was 1,000 rubles a packet.
I said, I'm gonna go there, take a job in the tea factory.
I got a job with the tea factory and they would bring in the trains with the tea and I used to unload them, 100 pounds [INAUDIBLE].
There was a wall there and a police around the wall-- nobody should steal anything.
Because it was very valuable, the tea.
Tea.
And-- and-- and I said to the chief of the police, what do-- what do you think?
I take 100 pound bag and I throw across the-- the wall.
And you stay there.
When I get up, I pick it up and we go home and the invalids who had come back from the war with legs, with everything, they were in the black market.
They were waiting in the house to buy it.
Anyway, soon I got finished work, I go over there--
The guards who the police was there waiting for me so nobody should take it.
When I finished work, I took the bagged tea on my shoulder, and he was walking with me with a bayonet like he arrest me and nobody should touch me.
Instead of going to the police station, he went to my house and there, the-- the people were waiting.
My brother didn't know about it.
He came to visit me and looked in the window and see police in my house.
He give it to me, don't tell them I'm your brother, and I don't want no-- they know me.
He was so afraid for the [? shadow. ?] And-- and I-- if not from me, I supply them-- my nieces got the-- sick, the typhoid, they almost died, food.
And I did everything for them.
When I got there, I-- I supply with meat, with everything to eat.
If not, they would die.
After the war--
But he was working.
He was working.
He was working, he made a salary, but he would not take anything--
--out from the factory.
No.
But-- oh, his money that he made wasn't enough to--
He made-- he made for a month's money he could spend for a day on food and [INAUDIBLE].
And I stayed with my brother there till the-- till the-- the war was almost over.
And I-- and I went back to Poland.
I went back to Poland.
The first thing I-- I landed in Lwow.
No, no, 1944, the end of '44, I went back from Samarkand to Poland.
I had a ticket.
I had money.
I bought a ticket and I went back to Blonie.
And I came back there from the border.
I took a train and I went to Warsaw.
And the Polacks saw me in Warsaw, they saw a ghost.
You're alive?
How'd you survive?
Yeah.
I know them--
--my friends.
They-- they were thinking I went to the oven with my brother from the Warsaw ghetto.
They don't know I was a prisoner in Russia.
And they said, hey, you're alive?
I said, sure.
And-- and I meant to my town, to Blonie.
Poles, yes.
I went to-- to-- to-- to my city, and I-- and I got the news of what they did to the family, what they-- how they took them to the ghetto in Warsaw and how they died and everything.
The house where I used to live, was somebody else there.
But I was friendly with the priest in Poland, where family was very, very-- I used to-- we are like my friends.
And I said, I'm going to the church and see the priest, talk to him what happened to my family, [? your ?] family.
I came in was two Polacks sitting there, two Polacks.
And on said, who are you?
I said, I was born here, live here.
He took out the uniform with the garb as a priest.
I said, I want to see the priest.
He said, come.
I take you to the sanctuary.
He's there.
I come in there.
He went out for another city, the priest.
But the woman who worked there.
He said-- I called the-- the-- the woman who works in the church, she said, who are you?
I said, Galek.
My God, I remember you were a little boy.
Come in and have a coffee and everything.
I said, I'm not hungry.
She hugged me and kissed me.
And she tells me this story, too.
And later when I went to the police station-- I went to the police station, and I'm waited for them to give me a pass.
I want to go a pass back to-- to Krakow.
I already--
Krakow was not destroyed.
It was a city.
All the people settled there and I had a friend, [INAUDIBLE], who lived in Krakow.
And he had apartments.
He said, you come back to me, to Krakow, I give you an apartment and put in business.
And I'm standing there in the police station waiting to sign my free ticket.
I said to them, where is the police chief?
He says, here.
What?
He was a [? thief. ?] I-- unbelievable.
I was-- I was-- he was no good.
He was a [INAUDIBLE]?
Yes, anything.
He's the police.
He said to me, look, don't say nothing.
I make you the police chief here.
Stay here.
I said, go to hell.
Sign my-- my pass.
He wants me to stay in Blonie to-- to work for the Communists and become the police chief and everything.
No.
I went back to Krakow.
And the guy--
Oh, yes.
I met people and they told me-- I went to the post office.
They told me a Galek was there.
A Galek was there.
I think my brother who survived, he was there.
But anyway, I had one thing--
Yeah.
Yes.
People lived there.
Sure.
But somebody else already occupied it.
No, no, no.
What?
Oh, I-- I it-- my-- my-- my cousin had it, who survived, some pictures that have it, a little snapshot.
I had the snapshot, too.
I don't know what happened.
And-- and-- and my niece here made-- made-- made the copies.
My-- my-- my cousin, yes, has the picture.
And-- but anyway, I settled in Krakow.
In Krakow, they didn't know I was a Jew.
I speak Polish and Russian.
And the-- and I lived in a section, [INAUDIBLE].
If you know Krakow, the-- the Polish kings are buried there.
Anyway, they're preserved like Lenin was preserved.
They preserved the Polish kings.
And anyway, the-- the Polacks come over to me, you know the Jews came back from Russia?
They must have a lot of money.
Now let's go rob them and kill them.
Oh my God.
I said, what?
Why-- why you want to rob them?
How you know they got money?
Anyway, and I was shaking.
They don't know I was a Jew.
I was shaking.
Later on, I don't know [INAUDIBLE] in Moscow are the Russian army who surrendered the building and have an investigation what they did.
And this happened at a police made the-- made the-- tried to organize the killing.
The police.
Tried to organize the killing of?
To go in and rob the Jews and kill them.
Yes.
But the Russian Army surrendered the building and American government in Germany gave the Polish government an order, you cannot control your people.
Give the people a right to move and get out from there.
Open the borders and let them go.
And we got two weeks to pack and go-- and go.
And the men who lived there were--
No, no, no.
No.
There-- there-- there were-- the police got announcements.
Anybody who wants to leave, the mayor of the city-- it's a beautiful city--
Krakow.
Yes.
And he said, you can leave but you have to leave everything what you have.
Just take your anything.
We-- we-- we crossed the border and we went by-- to a train to Czechoslovakia.
In the train, we were not allowed to speak Russians.
The Russian soldiers were there, the train, Czechoslovakia.
I-- I had a-- a-- I had a-- a Polish birth certificate and a passport.
I had a Krakow passport.
And we landed in [?
Austria-Germany. ?] And [?
Austria-Germany, ?] we were there a day or two--
Well, that was already 1945 then.
In Austria we landed, in Austria.
In Austria, we stayed there for a week and they put us--
We went to the camps in Germany.
What?
With all the people.
People who-- who in the train-- with people.
No.
Yeah.
In the [INAUDIBLE] camp.
And we went to a camp in [INAUDIBLE], that's near Frankfurt.
The-- the-- the German military [INAUDIBLE], a big place there.
And they took over and they put us--
In-- in Stuttgart-- in Frankfurt.
Yes.
In '45, yes.
When-- when I was-- left Poland was in '45 and went to Austria and there they put us in-- in this camp.
In this camp, I was volunteered to be a chief cook.
I was their cook and we formed a soccer team.
Yeah.
A soccer team.
And when I arrived to play soccer in Ulm, it's in-- near Stuttgart.
My friends were there who was with me in Poland, soccer players and everything.
They said, you gonna stay there?
You come with us.
We settle you in a camp with the people who had the numbers.
They called them [POLISH].
They were the [POLISH].
There was a camp, a beautiful camp [INAUDIBLE] they took all from the Germans.
I said, no.
You go back to Frankfurt.
We go with you, take your [INAUDIBLE] and you settle here.
And I settled there in Heidenheim.
Heidenheim.
This is between Ulm and Stuttgart, a beautiful city.
And there I played soccer.
The-- the owner supplied the food and everything.
Yeah.
United Nations supplied the food.
Later on, they camped in Austria-- Australia.
People, to-- to-- when they liquidated the camps, everybody-- the Catholics at the church guarantee it here in America with the jobs, this, they bring the Catholics, the Russians.
And they-- and the HIAS took the Jews.
And I said, I don't want to go to Australia, build in Australia.
I want to go to America.
And I came-- and I came, and the HIAS took me to America.
On, no.
I-- I-- I stayed to the last.
I-- I-- I-- I-- I stayed to the last.
I didn't want to go no place if-- if the HIAS going to take me to America, I go.
And later on, they-- they-- they had--
Oh, yes.
I registered with them.
And they had signatures here from the studio, the photographer where I worked.
He signed that he has the job for me.
Every factory that we went there, how many people you're gonna supply, how many people you're going to get.
We're going to give them a home, but you give them a job.
All over Germany.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
I playing for every camp.
Yeah.
Ukraine, Polacks, every camp we played.
Yeah.
Against, yeah.
We played with the Polacks, we got orders, if you lose the game, you're not going to get the-- the food [? for ?] [? you ?] [? get ?] [? it. ?] We-- we just like a war when we played with them.
But we won, one to nothing.
Look, it's anything-- when I-- when I came to America, I worked very hard, day and night.
I didn't speak English.
What?
Munich.
No.
I came--
No, no.
Just-- just for-- in Poland, I didn't get money, either.
We played for glory.
For the city.
We-- we-- here in America it's money.
No, but sometimes people pay you or sometimes you had a job.
No, no, no.
No.
Here when I came to New York, the Vienna [INAUDIBLE].
You know [INAUDIBLE]?
[INAUDIBLE], that was a Jewish group.
They-- they-- they were the best soccer from Austria, in Vienna.
And I know them, some people there.
And I went up to them, I want to play in the team.
In New York.
And they say, we're going to pay you for a game.
I said, no way.
I want a job.
I want a security and home.
I have my father and mother.
Here when I get injured, who's gonna pay for me?
No, we're going to pay you for-- I said, forget about it.
By game.
For me, it was no good.
In '49, September of 1949.
Oh, no.
My brother went to Israel.
He was in Germany, too, my-- my-- my brother.
And the older brother, the HIAS took him here.
Oh, after the war, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not anywhere.
I find them in the war.
I find them in Samarkand.
Oh, right.
Right.
That brother.
But the other brother.
Yeah.
Oh, the other was-- I never find him.
You found two brothers survived.
The two brothers were in Russia.
Oh.
They both were in Samarkand.
I thought one.
Yeah.
I lived with them together.
Yeah.
Well, I was-- I was very, very excited.
I went there--
Yeah.
September-- September '49.
No.
The date, no.
It was September.
After three years.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was a-- oh.
